root_cause: The sensor readings indicate normal operating conditions, with all parameters within expected ranges, suggesting that there is no underlying issue or root cause for a fault since no fault has been detected. The tool wear reading of 5 minutes is relatively low, indicating that the tool is still in good condition and not likely to be the cause of any issues.

recommendations.Description: Keep a close eye on sensor readings to ensure that all parameters remain within expected ranges. This will help identify any potential issues before they become major problems.
Plan a routine inspection of the tool to verify its condition and replace it if necessary. Although the tool wear reading is currently low, regular inspections can help prevent unexpected downtime.

Review the current maintenance schedule to ensure it is aligned with the tool's condition and operating parameters. Update the schedule as needed to prevent over- or under-maintenance, optimizing resource allocation and minimizing downtime.

Figure 8: Speed proﬁles for surface waves (plotted as v√ρ) in steel subject to (a) uniaxial compressive
stress, with pre-stretch λ decreasing from 0.990 to 0.905 (on the left) and (b) pure shear stress, with
pre-stretchλ decreasing from 0.99 to 0.95 (on the right). As the color of the curves changes from green
to blue, λ is decreased by regular increments of 0.0056 from one curve to the next.
SAWs in deformed polystyrene behave in a more orderly way, as they travel at their
fastest along the direction of greatest stretch θ = 90◦ and at their slowest along θ = 0◦.
Although the ﬁrst derivative of the velocity proﬁle is not a monotone function of the
angle, no secondary extremum develops, in contrast to the behavior of the body waves
in the same material (see previous section).
Finally, SAW propagation in deformed hydrogel is also almost regular under uniaxial
compression even at a relatively large strain (up to 40%); see Figure 10(a). However, two
secondary extrema develop under pure shear stress, with the secondary minimum in an
oblique direction, eventually becoming an absolute minimum; see Figure 10(b).
6. Conclusion
Clearly, the existence of oblique slowest waves greatly complicates the determination of
the principal directions of strain in a deformed body. Finding the direction where a wave
travels at its slowest or fastest is not a guarantee of having determined the direction of
greatest compression or tension, or that it is indeed a principal direction. In our examples,
